request.question: Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner , he is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space, was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by who?.

title: Patrick Topping
Steve Lawler
Maceo Plex
Richie Hawtin
Sven Väth
Jamie Jones (DJ)
Space (Ibiza nightclub)
Roger Sanchez
Luciano (DJ)
Tech house
sentences: Patrick Topping (born 25 February 1989) is a British, Newcastle born, DJ and producer.
With releases on Hot Creations, Hottrax and Relief Records Patrick has hit Number 1's on the Beatport Tech House charts, and received DJ Awards for Newcomer DJ and Track Of The Season in Ibiza.

Steve Lawler is a British house music producer and DJ born in Birmingham, England he is a five time DJ Awards winner and has held residences at many popular dance clubs such as Space, The End and Twilo.
Because of his successful performances at Space in Ibiza, he was nicknamed "King of Space".
Lawler has released several mix albums and is especially well known for his "Lights Out" series featured on Boxed.
He currently runs the record label VIVa MUSiC.
He also founded the now defunct Harlem Records.
Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner from Dallas and Miami, United States, origin of Cuba.
He now resides in Barcelona, Spain with his wife and kids.
He is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space and Amnesia.
Richard "Richie" Hawtin (born June 4, 1970) is a Canadian electronic musician and DJ and three-time DJ Awards winner.
He was an influential part of Detroit techno's second wave of artists in the early 1990s and has been a leading exponent of minimal techno since the mid-1990s.
He is best known for his work under the alias Plastikman and for his ENTER.
events in Ibiza and around the world.
Sven Väth (born 26 October 1964) is a German DJ/producer and three time DJ Awards winner whose career in electronic music spans well over 30 years.
His single, “Electrica Salsa” under the alias OFF launched his career in 1986.

Lovingly referred to as “Papa Sven” by his fans, Sven Väth has made his mark in the music community by being one of Germany’s “pop stars” in the nineties, running two famous night clubs in Germany, and starting his own company Cocoon, which encompasses a booking agency, record label, and a branch for events.

He is recognized for cultivating the underground electronic music scene not just in Germany but in Ibiza as well, with his own night at Amnesia for fourteen years and after-parties at creative locations around the island.
Sven Väth is a major proponent of vinyl, using only two decks and a mixer for his extensive DJ sets, his longest set having been 30 hours.
Jamie Jones is a Welsh DJ, producer and two time DJ Awards winner.
He is a member of the electronic music group Hot Natured and the head of the record label Hot Creations, as well as its sub-labels Emerald City and Hottrax.
Jones was voted the top DJ of 2011 by the online dance music magazine Resident Advisor.
In 2012 he received mainstream success with Hot Natured's single "Benediction", which reached number 31 in the UK Singles Chart.
Jones hosts his own "Paradise" party at Ibiza's DC10 nightclub.
In early 2016 Jones launched The House of Hot, an online shop clothing line, vinyls and artwork.
Space ( , ] , ] ) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by STANCA.
It was awarded "Best Global Club" at the International Dance Music Awards in 2005, 2006, 2012, and again in 2013.
"Space" was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, close to the airport.
Roger Rene Sanchez (born June 1, 1967) is an American house music DJ, Remixer and Grammy Award Winner for his remix of "Hella Good" by No Doubt in 2003.
He is a four time DJ Awards winner for "Best House DJ" in 1999, 2002, 2004 and 2007 and has received twelve nominations in all.
He won the first International Dance Music Award for Best Podcast in 2007 and has received 8 IDMA nominations for Best American DJ (2003–2010).
Luciano (born Lucien Nicolet on February 24, 1978 in Montcherand, Switzerland) is a Chilean-Swiss DJ and producer he is a five-time DJ Awards winner and ten-time nominee.

He is part of a small group of people who were responsible for developing and nurturing the underground electronic music scene in Chile, via promoting, playing, and throwing parties in the Chilean club circuit.
His DJ sets are strongly influenced by Latin American music, a sound he heavily incorporates.
Over the years, Luciano's initial style of minimal techno has developed and evolved into a blend of house and techno.
Luciano has been constantly involved in various music projects, such as playing live performances, running his label Cadenza Records, and hosting multiple parties in Ibiza.
Tech house is a subgenre of house music that mixes elements of techno with house.

The term "tech house" developed as a shorthand record store name for a category of electronic dance music that combined musical aspects of techno, such as "rugged basslines" and "steely beats," with the harmonies and grooves of progressive house.
The music originally had a clean and minimal production style that was associated with techno from Detroit and the UK.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner , he is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space, was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by who?.
hop1_docs: Maceo Plex | Eric Estornel, better known by his stage names Maceo Plex and Maetrik, is an American DJ, producer and DJ Awards winner from Dallas and Miami, United States, origin of Cuba.  He now resides in Barcelona, Spain with his wife and kids.  He is best known for diverse production style, influenced primarily by house and techno but also tech house, as well as a longtime performer for many Ibiza-based clubs, including Space and Amnesia.

Space (Ibiza nightclub) | Space ( , ] , ] ) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by STANCA.  It was awarded "Best Global Club" at the International Dance Music Awards in 2005, 2006, 2012, and again in 2013.  "Space" was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, close to the airport.

Steve Lawler | Steve Lawler is a British house music producer and DJ born in Birmingham, England he is a five time DJ Awards winner and has held residences at many popular dance clubs such as Space, The End and Twilo.  Because of his successful performances at Space in Ibiza, he was nicknamed "King of Space".  Lawler has released several mix albums and is especially well known for his "Lights Out" series featured on Boxed.  He currently runs the record label VIVa MUSiC.  He also founded the now defunct Harlem Records.

Luciano (DJ) | Luciano (born Lucien Nicolet on February 24, 1978 in Montcherand, Switzerland) is a Chilean-Swiss DJ and producer he is a five-time DJ Awards winner and ten-time nominee.  He is part of a small group of people who were responsible for developing and nurturing the underground electronic music scene in Chile, via promoting, playing, and throwing parties in the Chilean club circuit.  His DJ sets are strongly influenced by Latin American music, a sound he heavily incorporates.  Over the years, Luciano's initial style of minimal techno has developed and evolved into a blend of house and techno.  Luciano has been constantly involved in various music projects, such as playing live performances, running his label Cadenza Records, and hosting multiple parties in Ibiza.

Switch (house DJ) | David James Andrew Taylor, better known his stage name Switch, is a British DJ, songwriter, sound engineer, and record producer.  He is best known for his work with M.I.A..  In the fidget house genre, Switch runs his own music label "Dubsided", as well as the label Counterfeet, established in 2006 with fellow producer Sinden.  He has released various singles under his own name, and is also well known for remixing and producing for many major artists.  He is a former member of the American electronic dancehall group Major Lazer.

Onur Sasmaz | Onur Sasmaz, commonly known by his stage name DJ Onur or DJ Sasmaz, is a Turkish DJ/producer.  He is best known for the live performing.  Starting out with techno and subsequently moving to hardstyle, they eventually worked in other EDM styles including progressive/electro house.

Thomas Gandey | Thomas Gandey is an English electronic music producer, vocalist, keyboard player and DJ born on 21 March 1976, known for work under his own name as well as under his "Cagedbaby" pseudonym.  He has remixed the likes of Shirley Bassey, Ocean Colour Scene, Empire of the Sun, Fatboy Slim, The Temper Trap and Grace Jones and DJed and performed at many of the world's best-known clubs and festivals, including Womb, Glastonbury Festival, Fuji Rock Festival, and Tokyo's Big Beach Festival.  He has been a resident at We Love Space Ibiza and Barcelona's Razzmatazz.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Maceo Plex / Eric Estornel: passage [1] — quoted tokens: "Eric Estornel"; "Maceo Plex and Maetrik"; "including Space" (passage [1]).
- Space (Ibiza nightclub): passage [2] — quoted tokens: "was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza"; "from 1986 to 2016"; "owned by STANCA." (passage [2]).

2) Ambiguities:
- Only one candidate Space page appears in the passages: "Space (Ibiza nightclub)" ([2]). 
- The owner token "STANCA" appears in passage [2], but no separate passage/page for "STANCA" is provided here (owner page/details missing).
- No alternative owners or variants are shown in the supplied passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: Space (Ibiza nightclub) (as named in passage [2]).
- Missing fact needed: the authoritative owner/operator name and supporting details for Space (Ibiza nightclub) — i.e., verify whether "STANCA" is the official owner/operator (full legal name, time period of ownership, and a reliable source).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Space (Ibiza nightclub)" page — check the lede, infobox, and "History"/"Ownership" sections for owner/operator information and citations. (Search keywords: "Space Ibiza owner", check lede/infobox and Ownership/History.)
- Open "Maceo Plex" (Eric Estornel) page — check lede/Residency or Career section to confirm performances/residencies at Space (to confirm relevance).
- Open/search for a page titled "STANCA" or company profile (or check references cited on the Space page) — verify the corporate identity and that it is indeed the owner.
- If "STANCA" page not found, follow Space page references or search web/secondary sources via "Space Ibiza owned by STANCA" to confirm and get citation details.

Do not provide the final answer yet — fetch the pages/sections above and confirm the owner information before concluding.
hop2_docs: Space (Ibiza nightclub) | Space ( , ] , ] ) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza, Spain from 1986 to 2016, owned by STANCA.  It was awarded "Best Global Club" at the International Dance Music Awards in 2005, 2006, 2012, and again in 2013.  "Space" was located in Playa d'en Bossa on the outskirts of Ibiza Town, close to the airport.

Ibiza (town) | Ibiza (Catalan: "Eivissa" ] ; Spanish: "Ibiza" ), also known as Ibiza Town, is a city and municipality located on the southeast coast of the island of Ibiza in the Balearic Islands autonomous community.

Jesús (Ibiza) | Nostra Senyora de Jesús is a village on the northern side of Ibiza Bay, On the Spanish island of Ibiza, Spain.  The village is in the municipality of Santa Eulària des Riu and is located on the designated road PMV 810.  The village is 2.0 mi North of Ibiza Town and 6.7 mi of Ibiza Airport.

Ibiza, Ibiza | Ibiza, Ibiza is a 1986 Welsh comedy television film which aired on Welsh-language channel S4C.  It was directed by Ronw Protheroe and stars Caryl Parry Jones, Siw Hughes, Emyr Wyn, and Huw Chiswell.  It covers the stories of three women as they go on holiday to the island of Ibiza.

Pacte Progressista | The Pacte Progressista (English: Progressive Pact , Pacte), officially the Pacte Progressista d'Eivissa (English: Progressive Pact of Ibiza ) was an electoral alliance formed in Ibiza by the Socialist Party of the Balearic Islands, United Left of Ibiza, The Greens, Entesa Nacionalista i Ecologista (English: Nationalist and Ecologist Agreement of Ibiza ) and Republican Left of Catalonia to contest the 1999 and 2003 regional elections.  Previously, it had contested the 1996 Senate election in both Ibiza and Formentera as the Ibiza and Formentera in the Senate (Catalan: "Eivissa i Formentera al Senat" ).

Ibiza Weekender | Ibiza Weekender is a British reality television series following a group of 18-24 year olds spending a weekend at a hotel resort on the island of Ibiza, Spain.  Previously, the show was named The Magaluf Weekender and was located at Lively Hotel in Magaluf, located on the Balearic Island of Mallorca, from Series 1–3.  In Series 4, the show relocated to Ibiza, and was renamed "Ibiza Weekender".

SE Eivissa-Ibiza B | Societat Esportiva Eivissa-Ibiza B was a football team based in Ibiza Town in the autonomous community of Balearic Islands.  Founded in 2005, it plays in the Preferente.  It was the reserve team of UD Ibiza-Eivissa.
response.summary_2: owned by STANCA. Space (Ibiza nightclub) was a Superclub on the island of Ibiza from 1986 to 2016, located in Playa d'en Bossa.
